articles.title: 'The Era of Experience' Has an Unsolved Technical Alignment Problem
articles.summary: Steven Byrnes pushes back on Silver & Sutton's optimistic "Era of Experience" proposal — that future AI agents can reach superhuman capability by learning predominantly from experience via RL. Byrnes argues the technical alignment problem for powerful RL agents is far more unsolved than the paper implies: nobody has specified a reward function whose optimization by a superintelligent agent wouldn't be catastrophic, and the field's confident assertions obscure how wide this gap actually is.
takeaways: RL-based AGI presents categorically different alignment challenges than LLMs — the machinery for goal-directed optimization makes misalignment more dangerous
There is no known reward function that, if optimized to superhuman levels, would reliably lead to good outcomes rather than specification gaming
Optimism about technical alignment solutions tends to paper over the actual mechanisms — understanding precisely why RL agents are hard to align is more valuable than reassuring narratives

articles.title: The Chinese Civil Examinations
articles.summary: Hilde De Weerdt rehabilitates the Chinese civil examination system, pushing back against the early 20th-century dismissal of it as ossified and anti-creative. She shows how the system was a sophisticated mechanism for negotiating educational standards and social mobility across dynasties — and that the eight-legged essay, far from being a rote exercise, was evaluated by examiners for originality, creativity, and intentional departures from rigid parallelism.
takeaways: The modernist critique of Chinese civil exams was largely a political move by reformers, not a sober historical assessment — the exams actually rewarded creativity within structure
Imperial governments deliberately standardized exam genres without prescribing curricula, creating space for literati networks to negotiate intellectual standards across centuries

Social mobility through examination was real (37–50% of graduates came from non-bureaucratic families) but coexisted with persistent social reproduction — family networks shaped outcomes even in a formally meritocratic system

articles.title: The First Non-Opioid Painkiller
articles.summary: An account of the 27-year, multi-billion-dollar development of suzetrigine (Journavx), the first non-opioid analgesic approved for acute pain in decades. Rather than suppressing the brain's pain perception like opioids, suzetrigine selectively blocks NaV1.8 — a specific sodium channel in peripheral pain neurons — preventing the pain signal from being sent in the first place. The piece covers the mechanism, the failed molecules along the way, and the clinical trial design challenges.
takeaways: Suzetrigine works bottom-up: it blocks pain signal transmission in peripheral neurons via NaV1.8 selectivity, rather than top-down opioid receptor suppression in the brain — this is why it lacks addiction and withdrawal profiles

The development illustrates how ion channel selectivity was the key bottleneck — building a molecule that targets NaV1.8 without touching NaV1.4 (which causes heart arrhythmias) took decades of iterative chemistry

Clinical trials for painkillers rely heavily on surgical models (bunionectomy, abdominoplasty) performed mostly on women, creating systematic demographic gaps in the evidence base for chronic pain treatment

articles.title: Interpreting Polygenic Prediction of Cognitive Ability
articles.summary: Tyler Cowen flags a new paper by Tobias Wolfram et al. that makes a technically precise case for direct genetic effects on cognitive ability. Key result: within-family attenuation is ~0.88, meaning about 88% of the polygenic score's predictive power survives sibling controls and reflects direct genetic rather than environmental confounds. When corrected for measurement error in brief IQ tests, within-family associations with latent general ability reach ~0.45.
takeaways: The within-family design (comparing siblings) is the gold standard for ruling out environmental confounds — an attenuation of 0.88 is unusually high and suggests the predictive signal is mostly genetic, not just correlated with family background

Cross-ancestry portability at ~66% retention in African Americans matches theoretical expectations under a polygenic model — this is quantitatively meaningful for the debate about whether PGS findings generalize across populations

Polygenic scores for cognitive ability predict not just IQ but educational attainment, occupational status, and reduced cardiometabolic disease risk — the latent variable is doing real work across multiple life domains

articles.title: The Scent of Flavor
articles.summary: Linda Bartoshuk argues that Aristotle's five-sense model led scientists to miss a fundamental distinction: "flavor" is specifically retronasal olfaction — volatile compounds traveling from the mouth into the nasal cavity — not merely a blend of taste and smell. She traces how taste functions as a neural gating mechanism that modulates whether olfactory signals are perceived as flavor or ambient smell, and how this understanding opens pathways for reducing dietary sugar and sodium while maintaining palatability.
takeaways: Retronasal olfaction (smell from inside the mouth) and orthonasal olfaction (smell from outside) are processed differently by the brain — what we call "flavor" is a distinct sensory modality that classical sense-taxonomy obscured for two centuries

Taste gates flavor perception: blocking taste on one side of the tongue reduces retronasal olfactory sensation by 25%, and bilateral blocking causes 50% reduction — the two systems are neurologically coupled, not additive

Specific volatile compounds in tomatoes enhance perceived sweetness independently of sugar content — a finding with direct applications for formulating lower-sugar, lower-sodium foods without taste degradation

articles.title: The Enemy of the Good
articles.summary: Nicholas Clairmont makes a philosophical case against optimization as a general life posture. While acknowledging that optimization has genuine value in bounded domains (logistics, agriculture, engineering), he argues that applying it to intimate life — relationships, moral commitments, personal experiences — destroys precisely what makes those things worth having. Using Effective Altruism's reductio (prioritizing shrimp welfare over humans; advocating nuclear war to prevent AI) as his centerpiece, he argues consistent optimization logic reaches inhumane conclusions.
takeaways: The problem with optimization as a moral posture is not any individual premise but the mindset — the analytical detachment required to maximize corrodes the very attachment that makes intimate life meaningful

Orwell's observation that "the essence of being human is that one does not seek perfection" is not anti-rational sentimentality but a recognition that vulnerability, loyalty, and imperfection are constitutive of valuable relationships — not bugs to be fixed

EA-style reasoning shows that consistent optimization logic, applied without remainder, systematically produces conclusions that override common moral intuitions — Clairmont reads this not as a win for careful reasoning but as a symptom of optimization's domain blindness

articles.title: Where Does Politics Take Place?
articles.summary: Ege Yumusak argues, drawing on Rancière and Arendt, that authentic politics doesn't happen in formal deliberative spaces but in everyday moments where people contest each other's worldviews face-to-face. Liberalism's confinement of politics to rational public discourse while celebrating unlimited consumer choice creates citizens who are free but "uninformed, undisciplined, and mentally and emotionally stunted." Real politics happens when people make disagreement visible and transform what counts as a public concern.
takeaways: Rancière's distinction between "politics" (making the invisible visible, creating new possibilities) and "police" (the order that negates opposing worldviews as illegitimate) reframes what counts as genuine political engagement

Liberalism's model of deliberative rationality for public discourse, combined with unlimited private choice, paradoxically produces political disengagement — the disciplines required for public reasoning are never exercised in everyday life

Arendt's insight that political judgment emerges through ordinary citizens contesting common objects suggests that the site of politics is not institutions but the texture of social life — an uncomfortable challenge to both technocratic and proceduralist views
